---
title: "Urbanicity Index for Anthropological Field Sites"
author: "Tyler Barrett, Lev Kolinski, Marina Watowich, Amanda Lea, and Melanie Martin"
date: "`r Sys.Date()`"
output:
  html_document:
    toc: true
    toc_depth: 2
    toc_float: true
    theme: flatly
---

# Introduction

This tutorial demonstrates how to compute and visualize urbanicity metrics for anthropological field sites using the `pecahnurbanicity` package, developed as part of the [**Population Ecology, Aging, and Health Network (PEcAHN)**](https://sites.duke.edu/pecahn/).

These tools integrate multiple open-access datasets, including OpenStreetMap (OSM) infrastructure data, raster-based friction surfaces, population density grids, and nighttime light imagery. The package supports multi-year analysis to track urbanization trends over time.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Installation

You can install the development version of **pecahnurbanicity** directly from GitHub:

``` r
# install.packages("devtools")
devtools::install_github("tmbarrett2/pecahn-urbanicity/pecahnurbanicity")
```

Then load the package:

``` r
library(pecahnurbanicity)
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Required Data

Raster data are pulled **directly from Google Earth Engine (GEE) at runtime**. To run the geospatial measures you need:

-   A **Google Earth Engine account** ([sign up at earthengine.google.com](https://earthengine.google.com/))
-   A **Google Cloud Project** with the Earth Engine API enabled
-   The [`rgee`](https://r-spatial.github.io/rgee/) package installed and authenticated (see **Setup** below)

The datasets accessed on Earth Engine are:

1.  **Friction surface** (for travel-time measures): Malaria Atlas Project Global Friction Surface 2019, walking-only (`projects/malariaatlasproject/assets/accessibility/friction_surface/2019_v5_1_walking_only`).\
    *Reference:* Weiss et al. (2020). *Nature Medicine*, 26(12), 1835–1842. DOI: [10.1038/s41591-020-1059-1](https://doi.org/10.1038/s41591-020-1059-1)

2.  **Population density** (for multi-year population estimation): WorldPop Global Project Population (`projects/sat-io/open-datasets/WORLDPOP/pop`, annual 2015–2030).\
    *Reference:* WorldPop (www.worldpop.org — School of Geography and Environmental Science, University of Southampton)

3.  **Nighttime lights** (for multi-year light intensity measures): VIIRS DNB annual composites (`NOAA/VIIRS/DNB/ANNUAL_V22`, band `average_masked`).\
    *Reference:* Elvidge et al. (2021). *Remote Sensing*, 13(5), 922. DOI: [10.3390/rs13050922](https://doi.org/10.3390/rs13050922)

4.  **Settlement classification** (for the nearest urban center): GHSL Degree of Urbanisation settlement model, GHS-SMOD (`JRC/GHSL/P2023A/GHS_SMOD_V2-0`).\
    *Reference:* European Commission Joint Research Centre, GHSL project (P2023A).

**OpenStreetMap** data (roads, facilities, buildings) are queried live via the Overpass API and cached locally.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Setup

``` r
# Install dependencies if not already available
# install.packages(c("osmdata", "sf", "raster", "gdistance", "rgee", "ggplot2", "dplyr"))

# Load the package
library(pecahnurbanicity)
```

`pecahnurbanicity` has help documentation. For any function, you can use `?function-name` to bring up the documentation.

## Google Earth Engine setup

The geospatial raster measures pull data from Google Earth Engine through `rgee`. The first time you use the package you need to install the Earth Engine Python API and authenticate:

``` r
# Install rgee if not already installed
# install.packages("rgee")

# One-time setup: installs the EE Python API in an isolated environment
library(rgee)
ee_install(py_env = "rgee")  # run once; follow prompts for Miniconda if needed

# Authenticate with your Google account (opens a browser window)
ee_Initialize(project = "your-gcp-project-id")

# Verify setup
ee_check()
```

If you have already run `ee_install()` and `ee_Initialize()` in a prior session, you can start directly with `library(rgee); ee_Initialize(project = "your-gcp-project-id")`. The package will also initialize Google Earth Engine automatically the first time it needs raster data, as long as you pass your project via the `ee_project` argument (shown below).

## Site coordinate files

Set your local file path to where your site coordinates are stored:

``` r
fp <- "your_local_path/pecahn-urbanicity/test_data"
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Load Example Data

Two example datasets are provided:

1.  **Boundary-defined communities** (`test_data_boundaries.csv`)\
    Communities with fully specified bounding boxes (north/south/east/west coordinates).

2.  **Point-defined communities** (`test_data_single_point.csv`)\
    Communities defined by a single central point and a user defined radius.

``` r
test_data_boundaries <- read.csv(file.path(fp, "test_data_boundaries.csv"))
test_data_single_point <- read.csv(file.path(fp, "test_data_single_point.csv"))
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Create Bounding Boxes

Use `create_bounding_boxes()` to generate bounding boxes for each community.

For optimal results, create **two sets of bounding boxes**:
- **Local bounding boxes** (1-2 km) for community-level measures like population density, nighttime lights, and infrastructure counts
- **Regional bounding boxes** (5 km) for distance measures to facilities that may be further away

``` r
# Create local bounding boxes for community-level measures
bbox_1km <- create_bounding_boxes(test_data_single_point, distance_km = 1)

# Create regional bounding boxes for distance searches
bbox_5km <- create_bounding_boxes(test_data_single_point, distance_km = 5)

# Or use the same size for both if appropriate
bbox_boundaries <- create_bounding_boxes(test_data_boundaries)
```

-   The **local bounding box** defines the spatial extent for population density, nighttime lights, building density, and counts of local infrastructure (shops, financial services, transport stops, cell towers).
-   The **regional bounding box** defines the starting search area for distance measures (healthcare, schools, paved roads, urban centers). The search will expand from this area until facilities are found.
-   If your dataset includes only a central point, the function expands outward by the given radius (default: 5 km).

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Compute Urbanicity Measures

## Single-Year Analysis

For a single year of population and nighttime light data:

``` r
# Replace "your-gcp-project" with your Google Cloud Project ID
ee_Initialize(project = "your-gcp-project")

test_results_single <- compute_urbanicity_iterative(
  local_bboxes    = bbox_1km,
  regional_bboxes = bbox_5km,
  metrics         = "all",
  ee_project      = "your-gcp-project",
  search_buffer   = 1,  # degrees of friction-surface padding; keep modest (the
                        # friction raster is pulled via a size-limited download URL)
  population_years      = 2025,
  nighttime_light_years = 2025,
  verbose = FALSE
)
```

**Note:** If you only have one set of bounding boxes, you can omit `regional_bboxes` and the function will use `local_bboxes` for all measures:

``` r
test_results_single <- compute_urbanicity_iterative(
  local_bboxes = bbox_5km,
  metrics      = "all",
  ee_project   = "your-gcp-project",
  population_years      = 2020,
  nighttime_light_years = 2020
)
```

## Multi-Year Analysis

To track urbanization trends over time, provide vectors of **years** for population and nighttime lights. The data for each year are pulled from Earth Engine automatically — no file paths needed.

``` r
# Compute multi-year urbanicity metrics
test_results_multiyear <- compute_urbanicity_iterative(
  local_bboxes    = bbox_1km,
  regional_bboxes = bbox_5km,
  metrics         = "all",
  ee_project      = "your-gcp-project",
  search_buffer   = 1,
  population_years      = c(2015, 2017, 2019, 2021, 2023, 2025),
  nighttime_light_years = c(2015, 2017, 2019, 2021, 2023, 2025),
  verbose = FALSE
)
```

The resulting time-varying columns are named after the requested years:
- `pop_density_2015`, `pop_density_2017`, ..., `pop_density_2025`
- `nighttime_light_2015`, `nighttime_light_2017`, ..., `nighttime_light_2025`

**Data coverage:** the default WorldPop next-generation collection covers 2015–2030 and VIIRS annual lights cover 2012–2025. For population years before 2015, override `population_asset = "WorldPop/GP/100m/pop"` (the official WorldPop product, 2000–2021).

## Subset of Metrics

You can also compute only selected metrics:

``` r
test_results_subset <- compute_urbanicity_iterative(
  local_bboxes    = bbox_1km,
  regional_bboxes = bbox_5km,
  metrics         = c("roads", "shops", "hospital", "schools", "population", "nighttime_light"),
  ee_project      = "your-gcp-project",
  search_buffer   = 1,
  population_years      = c(2015, 2020),
  nighttime_light_years = c(2015, 2020)
)
```

## Urban Center: GHSL Degree of Urbanisation

The nearest **urban center** is identified using the GHSL Degree of Urbanisation settlement model (GHS-SMOD) on Google Earth Engine. The function finds the nearest GHS-SMOD urban cell and reports least-cost travel time to it (`travel_time_urban_center_min`).

Two arguments control this:

-   `urban_center_codes` — which GHS-SMOD `smod_code` classes count as an "urban center". The default is `c(23, 30)` (Dense Urban Cluster **and** Urban Center). Use `30` for strict Urban Center only.
-   `ghsl_year` — the GHS-SMOD epoch (5-year epochs from 1975 to 2030; default `2020`). Non-epoch years are snapped to the nearest available epoch.

``` r
# Strict Urban Center only (smod_code == 30), 2020 epoch
res_strict_urban <- compute_urbanicity_iterative(
  local_bboxes    = bbox_1km,
  regional_bboxes = bbox_5km,
  metrics         = "all",
  ee_project      = "your-gcp-project",
  urban_center_codes = 30,
  ghsl_year          = 2020,
  population_years      = 2020,
  nighttime_light_years = 2020
)
```

For remote field sites with no nearby formal Urban Center, the wider default (`urban_center_codes = c(23, 30)`) makes it more likely that a nearby urban cell is found before the progressive search is exhausted (see the boundary-distance flags below).

## Large Datasets (>5 Locations)
If computing urbanicity metrics on a dataset containing information for many communities (e.g., >5 locations), it may be advisable to run the `compute_urbanicity_iterative()` function within a `for` loop to batch 5 communities at a time. This may help to avoid timeout issues related to accessing OSM data.

```r
# Example `for` loop for computing urbanicity metrics on dataset containing locations for many (e.g., >5) communities

# Create local bounding boxes for community-level measures
bbox_1km <- create_bounding_boxes(test_data_single_point, distance_km = 1)

# Create regional bounding boxes for distance searches
bbox_5km <- create_bounding_boxes(test_data_single_point, distance_km = 5)

# Count number of communities 
n <- length(bbox_1km)

# Create empty list object to store generated data
results_list <- list()

# Run `for` loop to process 5 communities at a time, storing generated dataframes within the `results_list` object. Dataframes will be named according to the community numbers they contain (e.g., 1_5, 6_10, etc.):

for (start_idx in seq(1, n, by = 5)) {
  
  end_idx <- min(start_idx + 4, n)
  idx_range <- start_idx:end_idx 
  
  message("Processing communities ", start_idx, " to ", end_idx)
  
  # Run urbanicity computation
  res <- compute_urbanicity_iterative(
    local_bboxes        = bbox_1km[idx_range],
    regional_bboxes     = bbox_5km[idx_range],
    metrics             = "all",
    ee_project          = "your-gcp-project",
    search_buffer       = 1,
    population_years      = 2020,
    nighttime_light_years = 2020,
    verbose = TRUE
  )
  
  # Store results
  results_list[[paste0(start_idx, "_", end_idx)]] <- res
}

# To merge batched dataframes into a single combined dataframe:

combined_df <- dplyr::bind_rows(results_list, .id = "batch")

```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Visualize Results

## Summary Plots

The `create_summary_plots()` function automatically generates summary plots for all numeric variables.

``` r
summary_plots <- create_summary_plots(test_results_multiyear)

# Example plots for infrastructure metrics
summary_plots$pct_paved_roads
summary_plots$travel_time_hospital_min

# Example plots for single-year time-varying metrics
summary_plots$pop_density_2020
summary_plots$nighttime_light_2020
```

## Temporal Trend Plots

For multi-year data, use `create_temporal_plots()` to visualize changes over time:

``` r
temporal_plots <- create_temporal_plots(test_results_multiyear)

# View population density trends
temporal_plots$pop_density

# View nighttime light intensity trends
temporal_plots$nighttime_light
```

## All Plots at Once

You can generate both summary and temporal plots simultaneously:

``` r
all_plots <- create_all_plots(test_results_multiyear)

# Access summary plots
all_plots$summary_plots$pct_paved_roads
all_plots$summary_plots$travel_time_hospital_min

# Access temporal plots
all_plots$temporal_plots$pop_density
all_plots$temporal_plots$nighttime_light
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Compute Urbanicity Composite Index

Once urbanicity metrics have been extracted with `compute_urbanicity_iterative()`, they can be combined into a single composite urbanicity score using `compute_urbanicity_composite_index()`. This function standardizes each of the six core features against the PEcAHN global reference distribution (1,400 globally stratified communities) and returns their row-wise mean as a composite index.

Scores are anchored to a fixed global scale, so they are directly comparable across study sites, datasets, and future data collection waves regardless of local sample size or composition.

## Basic Usage

``` r
results_indexed <- compute_urbanicity_composite_index(test_results_single)
```

This appends two columns to your data frame:

-   `urban_index` — the composite score. For the default z-score method, this is the mean number of standard deviations above or below the global reference mean across all six features. Higher values indicate greater urbanicity.
-   `n_features_used` — the number of non-missing features that contributed to each community's score. Communities with fewer than 6 non-missing features receive `NA` by default (see `min_features` below).

## Standardization Methods

Two standardization methods are available via the `method` argument:

``` r
# Z-score (default): scores are in SD units relative to the global reference mean
results_zscore <- compute_urbanicity_composite_index(test_results_single, method = "zscore")

# Min-max: scores are normalized to [0, 1] relative to the global reference range
results_minmax <- compute_urbanicity_composite_index(test_results_single, method = "minmax")
```

The z-score method is recommended for most analyses. It preserves the magnitude of differences between communities and allows scores to fall below zero (i.e., below the global reference mean), which is expected for rural field sites. The min-max method can be useful when a bounded [0, 1] scale is needed for reporting, but note that communities more rural than any site in the reference dataset will be clamped to 0 by default (see `clamp`).

## Multi-Year Data

For datasets with multiple years of nighttime light or population density, the function automatically uses the most recent available year for each variable:

``` r
# nighttime_light_2025 and pop_density_2025 are selected automatically
results_indexed <- compute_urbanicity_composite_index(test_results_multiyear)
```

The year in your data does not need to match the year used to build the reference parameters. Reference parameters for multi-year variables are stored under a year-agnostic key (`"nighttime_light"`, `"pop_density"`), so a dataset with `nighttime_light_2024` columns will standardize correctly against a reference built from `nighttime_light_2025`.

## Optional Arguments

``` r
results_indexed <- compute_urbanicity_composite_index(
  test_results_single,
  method            = "zscore",
  min_features      = 5,      # allow composite scores with as few as 5 features
  keep_standardized = TRUE,   # append per-feature _z columns for inspection
  verbose           = TRUE    # print per-feature diagnostics to console
)
```

-   **`min_features`** — communities with fewer non-missing features than this threshold receive `NA`. Lower this if your data have systematic missingness on one feature (e.g., no hospital within the search radius).
-   **`keep_standardized`** — appends per-feature standardized columns (suffixed `_z` for z-score, `_norm` for min-max) to the output, which is useful for diagnosing which features are driving composite scores.
-   **`clamp`** — (min-max only) if `TRUE` (default), values outside the global reference range are clamped to [0, 1] with a warning. Set to `FALSE` to allow out-of-range scores that preserve the relative position of extreme communities on the global scale.

## Inspecting the Reference Parameters

To see which reference parameters and source year columns are being used by the currently installed version of the package, call:

``` r
urbanicity_ref_params()
```

This prints a formatted table of per-feature means and SDs (z-score) or mins and ranges (min-max), along with the number of reference communities and the date the parameters were generated. This is useful for methods documentation and for verifying that the package was built against the expected version of the reference dataset.

## A Note on Boundary-Distance Estimates

Travel time features flagged as boundary-distance estimates (`travel_time_*_is_boundary_est == TRUE`) indicate that no feature was found within the maximum search area; the reported value is a lower bound, not a measured travel time (see **Output Interpretation** above). These communities will still receive a composite score, but their travel-time components reflect a censored observation. Consider whether to include them in downstream analyses or model them as right-censored.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Output Interpretation

Each column in the resulting data frame corresponds to a specific urbanicity indicator:

## Infrastructure Metrics (Time-Invariant)

| Variable | Description |
|---------------------------------|---------------------------------------|
| `pct_paved_roads` | Percent of total road length that is paved |
| `travel_time_hospital_min` | Estimated walking time (min) to nearest hospital |
| `travel_time_school_min` | Estimated walking time (min) to nearest school |
| `travel_time_urban_center_min` | Estimated walking time (min) to nearest urban center |
| `n_shops` | Number of commercial points of interest |
| `n_financial` | Number of financial institutions (ATMs, banks) |
| `n_transport_stops` | Number of public transport nodes |
| `n_cell_towers` | Number of mobile phone cell towers |
| `building_density` | Proportion of built-up land area (%) |

## Time-Varying Metrics (Multi-Year)

| Variable | Description |
|---------------------------------|---------------------------------------|
| `pop_density_YYYY` | Population density (per km²) for year YYYY |
| `nighttime_light_YYYY` | Mean nighttime light intensity for year YYYY |

## Boundary-Distance Flags

Each travel-time distance measure has a companion logical flag column:

| Variable | Description |
|---------------------------------|---------------------------------------|
| `travel_time_paved_road_is_boundary_est` | flag for the paved-road travel time |
| `travel_time_hospital_is_boundary_est` | flag for the hospital travel time |
| `travel_time_school_is_boundary_est` | flag for the school travel time |
| `travel_time_urban_center_is_boundary_est` | flag for the urban-center travel time |

A flag is `TRUE` when **no feature was found** within the maximum search area, so the reported travel time is a *boundary-distance estimate* (the mean travel time to the four edge-midpoints of the largest search box) rather than a measured time to a real feature. It is `FALSE` when a real feature was used, and `NA` when that metric was not computed.

Treat `TRUE` rows as **right-censored / lower-bound** observations — the true facility is at least as far as the reported value. In downstream analysis you may wish to exclude them, run a sensitivity analysis with and without them, or model them explicitly as censored.

These indicators can be aggregated or combined into composite indices of urbanicity, and the multi-year data enables longitudinal analysis of urbanization processes.

------------------------------------------------------------------------


# Citation

If you use this package in your research, please cite:

Barrett, T., Kolinski, L., Watowich, M., Lea, A., & Martin, M. (2025). pecahnurbanicity: Tools for computing urbanicity metrics for anthropological field sites. R package version 0.3.0. https://github.com/tmbarrett2/pecahn-urbanicity

------------------------------------------------------------------------
